coredata.dc:title: Cellular effects of low-intensity pulsed ultrasound and X-irradiation in combination in two human leukaemia cell lines
coredata.dc:creator: Buldakov, Mikhail A.
Hassan, Mariame A.
Jawaid, Paras
Cherdyntseva, Nadejda V.
Kondo, Takashi
coredata.dc:description: Previously, we have shown that a combination between X-irradiation and low-intensity pulsed ultrasound (US) could synergistically suppress cell survival post exposure (Buldakov et al., 2014). In this study, the cellular effects underlying the enhanced cell killing are investigated. U937 and Molt-4 cell lines were exposed to 1.0MHz US with 50% duty factor at 0.3W/cm2 and pulsed at 1, 5 and 10Hz immediately after exposure to X-rays at 0, 0.5, 2.5 and 5Gy. The cells were assayed at different time points to depict the major cellular events that culminated in cell death. For instance, membrane damage and cell lysis were estimated immediately following exposure and 24h later. Intracellular reactive oxygen species (ROS) were also determined flow cytometrically after treatment. Moreover, the extent of DNA damage and cell cycle progression were determined at 6 and 24h, respectively. Despite the general trend for synergism, there was a disproportionation of mediating factors depending on the cell type and its specific biological makeup. Immediately, US could induce appreciable necrotic cell death through extensive membrane damage in U937 but induced cell lysis in Molt-4 cells. ROS might have contributed to cell killing in Molt-4 but not in U937 cells. Although both of the physical modalities are significantly DNA-damaging alone, no additional damage was observed in combination. Moreover, override in some arrested cell cycle phases was also observed following combination. Collectively, the interaction between X-rays and US seems to depend mainly on the acoustic environment determined by the setup and this might explain the contradictory data among reports.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cell killing
Cell lysis
DNA damage
Ionizing radiation
Membrane damage
Ultrasound

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Assessment of membrane damage and/or cell death
Assessment of intracellular reactive oxygen species

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Propidium iodide (PI) uptake in U937 and Molt-4 cells assayed flow cytometrically (a) immediately after exposure (membrane damage) or (b) 24h later (cell killing). Data are presented as the mean±SEM of at least three independent triplicates.

Assessment of cell survival. (a) Illustration showing the possible time-dependent cell fate after exposure to ultrasound and/or X-rays. Boxes indicate cell categories with respect to cell survival and viability. Sp; surviving proliferating, Snp; surviving non-proliferating, NV; non-viable, L; lysed. The non-viable fraction before treatment is usually ⩽5%. Dotted circles enclose the treatment suggested as a cause to the respective effect (US; ultrasound, XR; X-irradiation), whereas the question marks indicate that available information is still insufficient to prove the contribution of the treatment in the observed effect. (b)–(d) The time-dependent distribution of cells treated with conditions that most likely produce cell lysis. Trypan blue dye exclusion test was performed at (b) 30min, (c) 6h, and (d) 24h post exposure to 5Gy, 1Hz-PRF US, or the combination. The decrease in total cell counts indicates cell lysis in (b) and the sum of dead and initially lysed cells in (c) and (d). Data are presented as the mean±SEM of at least three independent triplicates.

Intracellular reactive oxygen species (ROS) immediately after exposure in (a) U937 cells, and (b) Molt-4 cells. Open markers: single treatments; Closed markers: combined treatments. Data are presented as the mean±SEM of at least three independent triplicates. MFI; Mean Fluorescent Intensity.
Assessment of DNA damage. The extent of H2AX phosphorylation was determined 6h post treatment. Data are presented as the mean±SEM of at least three independent triplicates.
Cell cycle analysis 24h after treatment in (a) U937 cells and (b) Molt-4 cells. Data are presented as the mean±SEM of at least three independent triplicates.
Cell cycle phases of U937 cells assayed 24h after treatment with ultrasound and/or X-irradiation.
Cell cycle phases of Molt-4 cells assayed 24h after treatment with ultrasound and/or X-irradiation.

head.ce:title: Cellular effects of low-intensity pulsed ultrasound and X-irradiation in combination in two human leukaemia cell lines

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Radiological Sciences, Graduate School of Medicine and Pharmaceutical Sciences, University of Toyama, Toyama 930-0194, Japan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Graduate School of Medicine and Pharmaceutical Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Cancer Research Institute of Siberian Вranch of the Russian Academy of Medical Sciences, Tomsk, Russia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Cancer Research Institute of Siberian Вranch of the Russian Academy of Medical Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutics and Industrial Pharmacy, Faculty of Pharmacy, Cairo University, Kasr Al-Aini Str., Cairo 11562, Egypt

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Pharmaceutics and Industrial Pharmacy

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Radiological Sciences, Graduate School of Medicine and Pharmaceutical Sciences, University of Toyama, Sugitani 2630, Toyama 930-0194, Japan. Tel.: +81 (0)76 434 7265; fax: +81 (0)76 434 5190.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Previously, we have shown that a combination between X-irradiation and low-intensity pulsed ultrasound (US) could synergistically suppress cell survival post exposure (Buldakov et al., 2014). In this study, the cellular effects underlying the enhanced cell killing are investigated. U937 and Molt-4 cell lines were exposed to 1.0MHz US with 50% duty factor at 0.3W/cm2 and pulsed at 1, 5 and 10Hz immediately after exposure to X-rays at 0, 0.5, 2.5 and 5Gy. The cells were assayed at different time points to depict the major cellular events that culminated in cell death. For instance, membrane damage and cell lysis were estimated immediately following exposure and 24h later. Intracellular reactive oxygen species (ROS) were also determined flow cytometrically after treatment. Moreover, the extent of DNA damage and cell cycle progression were determined at 6 and 24h, respectively. Despite the general trend for synergism, there was a disproportionation of mediating factors depending on the cell type and its specific biological makeup. Immediately, US could induce appreciable necrotic cell death through extensive membrane damage in U937 but induced cell lysis in Molt-4 cells. ROS might have contributed to cell killing in Molt-4 but not in U937 cells. Although both of the physical modalities are significantly DNA-damaging alone, no additional damage was observed in combination. Moreover, override in some arrested cell cycle phases was also observed following combination. Collectively, the interaction between X-rays and US seems to depend mainly on the acoustic environment determined by the setup and this might explain the contradictory data among reports.

ce:section.ce:para: Radiation therapy (RT) is an approved and widely employed strategy for controlling cancer progression in clinic. RT is usually prescribed as curative, as part of adjuvant therapy regime along with chemotherapeutics, or as palliative to prevent recurrence of surgically removed tumors. Painless in itself, non-invasive, highly effective and of relative low-cost are some of the advantages of RT. Unfortunately, RT has been associated with severe adverse effects which could blow its merits away [2]. For this, the use of RT has been governed with a set of strict international regulations, and should be evaluated by case. Researchers, on the other hand, have been treading every road to optimize the use of X-rays in cancer treatment, i.e., to maximize cell killing and minimize side effects. On one side, great strides have been made in X-rays exposure modules as well as in its technology in order to better focus the beam to the tumor area and, meanwhile, decrease the exposure of the surrounding healthy tissues. Another way in optimization is the use of innovative combination strategies. Such combinations work on decreasing the dose of each modality while attaining similar (or possibly enhanced) cell killing rates. Among the suggested possibilities is the use of X-rays and low-intensity ultrasound.

Ultrasound (US) is a non-invasive, highly precise and deeply penetrating mechanical wave. Apart of its indispensable use in diagnosis, US potential to destroy tumor cells and induce cell killing has been reported on several occasions [3–5]. The biological effects are dependent on US parameters as well as the sonication setup. In most diagnostic applications, significant biological effects might not be observed owing to the strict settings of diagnostic equipment and the regulations for exposure procedures. In therapeutic setups, on the contrary, US can induce cell killing, or sensitize cells to forthcoming stresses. Researchers – decades ago – have thought of exposing cancer cells to X-rays and US seeking improvement in treatment outcomes. The studies on the combination can be broadly classified into two groups: One group employs US for its thermal effect, i.e., to induce a hyperthermic temperature rise, and another group operates under temperature controlled conditions. As “winds blow counter to what ships desire”, the reports on the combined effects of X-rays and US appeared as contradictory and inconclusive. For instance, while most of the former group investigations indicated a reduction in radiation dose required for a certain effect in combination experiments, they differed among each other in determining a unified protocol and order for applying both modalities [6,7]. On the other hand, under temperature controlled conditions, Clarke et al. showed the absence of synergism in his experimental setup regardless of the order of exposure to US being before, during, or after radiation [8]. In support, Fickweiler et al. stated that following a combined treatment of ionizing radiation and high-energy shock waves there was an additive or supra-additive interaction indicating weak mutual influence between the two modalities [9]. Loverock et al. stated that there was no synergism between US and X-rays at 37°C, but it occurs only when cells are heated although US mechanism might be non-thermal in its origin [10]. In contrast to the implied notion that US-thermal effect might be a key player for efficient combination, Jernberg et al. demonstrated that in absence of temperature rise US could synergistically decrease cell survival if applied before irradiation [11]. These studies, despite the obvious diversity in their conclusions, have identified a set of factors which contribute to the combination outcomes, e.g., the duration, dose, order of treatment as well as the initial biological impact of each treatment.

In a previous preliminary study, we have shown that the combination of relatively low doses of X-rays and low intensity pulsed US (at 1Hz-pulse repetition frequency (PRF)) resulted in synergistic increase in cell killing 24h post exposure in two human leukaemia cell lines [1]. Here, the study was expanded to include a 4×4 factorial experimental design to better assess the effect of radiation dose and acoustic environment in the interaction between the two modalities. Three PRFs were used in combination with increasing X-ray doses, namely, 1, 5 and 10Hz. Although the choices seem to be based on increasing PRF, i.e., modulating the pulse duration at constant energy input, for this in vitro setup the interpretation is different [12,13]. In fact, the three PRFs were chosen carefully to provide three different acoustic environments. At 50% duty cycle, the lower frequency (1Hz) produces longer pulses and greater fluid agitation. Cavitation and cell lysis are more likely to occur at this condition. On the other hand, 10Hz produces shorter pulses and the off-time between pulses is short enough to allow for standing waves to be established. Cavitation and cell lysis are held at minimum at 10Hz. At 5Hz, both liquid agitation and standing waves oppose each other creating an interim zone where the effect of US input energy can be observed without compromising cell viability due to environmental stresses [14]. Several analytical tests were performed to gain insights to some of the underlying major events.

Human myelomonocytic lymphoma U937 and acute lymphoblastic leukaemia Molt-4 cell lines were purchased from Human Sciences Research Resources Bank (Japan Human Sciences Foundation, Tokyo, Japan). The cells were maintained in RPMI 1640 medium (Gibco, Carlsbad, Japan), supplemented with 10% heat-inactivated fetal bovine serum (Gibco) at 37.0°C in humidified air with 5% CO2.

The ultrasonic generator (KUS-2S, Ito Chotanpa Co., Tokyo, Japan) emitting at a central resonant frequency of 1.0MHz and 50% of duty factor was used in this study. The effective output power measured by an ultrasound power meter (UPM-DT-10E, Ohmic Instrument Co., Easton, MD) was 1.68W corresponding to an output intensity of 0.3W/cm2. The modulation of the pulsed beam was in the frequency range from 1 to 100Hz [12]. For sonication, polystyrene culture dishes (diameter=35mm, base thickness=0.5mm, Corning Inc., Corning, NY) were placed in the near field directly above the planar circular transducer and perfectly centered with partially degassed water as the coupling agent [15,16]. Attenuation due to the culture dish was practically negligible (<10%). All experiments were carried out at room temperature. Sonication time was set to 30s and the net ultrasound energy for exposure was kept constant.

Irradiation was carried out as previously described [1]. The X-ray generator (MBR-150R-3, Hitachi Medico Technology Co., Kashiwa, Japan) operating at 150kV and 20mA at a dose rate of 5Gy/min determined by Fricke dosimetry was used. The cells were irradiated at room temperature at doses of 0.5, 2.5 or 5Gy.

For experiments, two ml of cell suspension were dispensed into 3.5cm culture dishes at a density of 1×106 cells/ml and left to rest in the incubator for 30min. Following X-irradiation, cells designated for combination treatment were exposed to US immediately, which means 5min after X-irradiation for transfer between rooms. All cells were kept at 37°C until analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assessment of membrane damage and/or cell death
ce:section.ce:para: Control and treated cells were harvested immediately or 24h after treatment and washed with cold phosphate buffer saline (PBS). The cell suspensions were allowed to incubate for 10min in the dark with propidium iodide (PI; Sigma Aldrich) (2μg/ml) at 4°C. Cells were then injected to a flow cytometer (Epics XL, Beckman Coulter, Miami, FL) and at least 10,000 events were analyzed (PI excitation/emission: 535/617nm).

Trypan blue dye exclusion test was performed by mixing 200μl of cell suspension initially containing 1×105 cells, with an equal amount of 0.3% Trypan blue solution in PBS. After 5min incubation at room temperature, the number of cells excluding Trypan blue (unstained) was counted using a microscope to estimate the number of intact viable and intact non-viable cells. The percentage of each fraction was calculated relative to the total number of cells in control. Cell lysis/loss was calculated as (the number of cells in the control – the sum of viable and non-viable cells in the sample).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assessment of intracellular reactive oxygen species

ce:section.ce:para: The histone H2AX becomes phosphorylated on serine 139 to form the so-called γH2AX in response to induced DNA double strand breaks where it acts as platform for spatiotemporal assembly of numerous proteins acting in response to DNA damage to form DNA checkpoints and facilitate DNA repair [19]. Here, we used the extent of H2AX phosphorylation as an indicator to DNA damage. Six hours after exposure, control and treated cells were harvested and washed with cold PBS. Cells were then fixed, permealized and finally incubated with FITC-conjugated anti-phospho-H2AX antibody according to the manufacturer’s instructions (H2A.X Phosphorylation Assay Kit, Millipore, Billerica, MA). The samples were finally run into the flow cytometer (FITC excitation/emission: 495/519nm).

Control and treated cells were collected, washed twice with cold PBS, and fixed with 70% pre-chilled ethanol overnight at −20°C. On the next day, samples were re-suspended in PBS containing RNAase (Nacalai Tesque, Kyoto, Japan) at a final concentration of 0.25mg/ml/1×106 cells for 30min at room temperature. Cell pellets were finally suspended in 50μg/ml PI/PBS staining solution, and incubated in the dark at 4°C for 20min before flow cytometric analysis. Samples were injected to the flow cytometer and 10,000 events were analyzed for DNA content as previously described [20]. Cell cycle analysis was performed 24h after treatment.

Experiments were performed in independent triplicates. All results are displayed as mean±standard error of the mean (SEM). Tests of significance were performed using two-way and one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) using GraphPad Prism 5 (GraphPad Software; San Diego, CA). One way ANOVA was followed by Tukey post hoc test for multiple comparisons (α =0.05) with p <0.05 considered to be statistically significant.

In our previous study, we have shown that the combined treatment of X-irradiation (0.5, 2.5 and 5Gy) and US (1Hz-PRF) resulted in synergetic cell killing of U937 and Molt-4 cells. Here, we have expanded our study to include several assessments of a number of end points in order to gain insights to the underlying events to cell killing. Moreover, more acoustic variables were employed to help better understanding of the determinants governing the successfulness of the combination regime. First, the effects on cell membrane, as the first line of defence, were speculated to contribute to the ultimate survival rates. Membrane Integrity was thus evaluated by PI uptake both immediately and 24h after exposure to different regimes. The statistical analysis of results and the evaluation of interaction between both exposure modalities showed that for both cell lines, US was responsible for most of the membrane damage observed immediately. Cell damage was more pronounced at 1Hz-PRF where fluid agitation and cavitational activity are high (Fig. 1 a). The immediate membrane damage in U937 cells increased significantly at all PRFs in combination with 0.5Gy, however, PRF dependence was observed at higher doses of radiation, as well as in Molt-4 cells at all radiation doses. The overall changes in immediate cell membrane disruption did not exceed 15% at any condition. In contrast, there was a dramatic increase in PI uptake 24h after combination treatments particularly in Molt-4 cells (Fig. 1b). At this time point, the statistical analysis revealed that US accounted for the PI uptake in U937 whereas in Molt-4 cells, the PI uptake was mainly attributed to X-irradiation. If this would hold true, it might be concluded that although the combination treatments might lead to similar outcomes in different cell lines, there could be disproportionation of roles determined by the initial sensitivities of cells to each treatment alone. Moreover, the absence of cells externalizing phosphatidylserine [1] suggests that the increase in cell death following the combination treatment might be accomplished through a non-apoptotic pathway. In all cases, the increase in cell membrane damage can explain in part the enhanced cell killing observed following the combination treatment.

Cell lysis is another important event to be addressed when physical stresses are considered. In US, the occurrence of inertial cavitation, where gaseous microbubbles collapse in the vicinity of cells, may blow up nearby cells. Other cells may experience extensive membrane damage depending on their distance from collapsing and/or oscillating microbubbles [21]. Cell lysis have been also observed with ionizing radiation [22,23]. Unfortunately, the flow cytometric assessment of PI uptake does not provide information on the extent of cell lysis unless specially adjusted as the percentages obtained are calculated from a fixed number of events. Microscopic examination of cells using Trypan blue dye provides a rich pool of information (Fig. 2 a). For instance, after treatment cells are expected to include a mixture of different populations, namely, Surviving (S; dye-excluding), and non-viable (NV; stained) cells; and the extent of cell lysis (L) can be calculated based on experimental controls. Moreover, Trypan blue dye exclusion test performed past a cell-doubling time can reflect the proliferative capacity of the cells in response to treatment. Surviving cells might retain their proliferating capacity (Sp) or they might come to a proliferative halt temporarily or permanently (Snp). If the treated cells are incubated for a period equivalent to one doubling period, all of the Snp, NV and L cells will account for the decrease in cell count, whereas the intact cells should include Sp, Snp and NV cells. Based on an estimate of the surviving cells, which remains the most important measure for the successfulness of a treatment, we have previously shown that 1MHz-US (0.3W/cm2, 50% DF and 1Hz-PRF could synergistically enhance cell killing induced by X-irradiation in human U937 and Molt-4 cells. Here, an estimate on the decrease of cell counts with time whether by lysis (immediate) or non-proliferation (delayed) is used to account for the enhanced cell killing (Fig. 2b–d). As shown in Fig. 2b, the immediate cell membrane damage was also higher in U937 cells compared to Molt-4 cells in agreement with PI results. Strikingly, U937 cells did not show any cell lysis at all exposure conditions, whereas some of Molt-4 cells were lost immediately after US as well as X-irradiation. Taken together, we may conclude that Molt-4 cells are more sensitive to US in terms of membrane damage and overall cellular strength. It has been reported that the status of the tumor suppressor protein p53 might affect the biophysical properties of cell membranes so that the cells lacking functional p53 would show lower fragility, reduced elasticity and higher deformability. These attributes might hinder membrane resealing post sonication but counteract cellular break up in U937 cells known to be p53 null [24,25].

Second, the role of reactive oxygen species (ROS) in enhanced cell killing was also evaluated. X-irradiation results in the formation of large amounts of free radicals in the medium proportional to radiation dose. On the other hand, free radicals are formed in an acoustic field when water sonolysis occurs in the microenvironment of a collapsing microbubble under the resulting high pressure and temperature, i.e., when inertial cavitation occurs [26]. The formed free radicals can generally react with essential biomolecules, including DNA, and alter their functionality and/or induce series of lesions that culminate in cell death [27]. Because the hydroxyl radicals (OH) are the most likely free radicals to form in an aqueous medium, we have used the ROS indicator APF, which possesses greater specificity and stability, to determine the extent of free radical formation immediately after exposure. For this study, the enhancement of cell killing through ROS formation was considered feasible only at 1Hz PRF, the condition at which inertial cavitation may occur [12]. Thus, samples sonicated at 1Hz following exposure to X-rays at 0.5, 2.5, or 5Gy were stained and analyzed flow cytometrically. As shown in Fig. 3 , US alone did not result in intracellular increase of OH radicals, unlike X-irradiation at 2.5 and 5Gy. X-irradiation induced more intracellular ROS in U937 cells than in Molt-4 cells. However, in combination treatment ROS increased in Molt-4 cells only. Therefore, ROS formation might justify in part for the synergistic cell killing in Molt-4 cells but not in U937 cells.

It is well known that the bioeffects of X-rays are mainly due to the induction of severe DNA lesions. On the other hand, we have recently shown that low-intensity US can induce DNA damage in vitro including the malicious DNA double strand breaks (DSBs) [28]. This DNA-damaging effect of US has been attributed to the mechanical activity of oscillating and/or collapsing gas cavities (cavitational effect) [29]. It has been speculated that a combination between X-irradiation and DNA-damaging US could promote the induction of DNA lesions and thus enhance cell killing. The evaluation of the extent of H2AX phosphorylation flow cytometrically revealed that although each treatment could induce DNA DSBs alone, upon combination there was no change from the highest induction by individual treatments (Fig. 4 ). This was in agreement with a study of the combination of X-irradiation and US (high intensity continuous waves) in which no enhancement in DNA damage beyond the highest induction by single modalities (US in their case) was observed no matter the cells were first sonicated or irradiated [11]. The authors attributed the lack of change to the failure of repair assuming statistically independent processes given that their assay was carried out immediately and the handling was intended to prevent repair artefacts in induction rate detection. In a study by Tsaponina and Chabes, the authors showed that the low-level pre-activation of the DNA damage-inducible proteins resulted in increasing damage tolerance to forthcoming DNA-damaging agents [30]. In Jernberg’s study, X-rays at a dose of 2Gy was a milder DNA damage-inducer compared to the high intensity continuous US applied [11]. In terms of the claimed damage tolerance, we would expect a lack of increase in cell killing when irradiation preceded sonication. In fact this postulation held true for they reported higher cell survival in clonogenic survival assays when cells were first irradiated. In our case, both modalities could induce comparable extents of DNA damage with US showing a lower trend. The pre-exposure of cells to X-irradiation must have activated the checkpoint proteins and thus no further damage could be observed at 6h post exposure to the combination treatment. Despite the fact that this phenomenon cannot be interpreted as increased tolerance unlike Tsaponina and Chabes, we are not sure how it can impact the cell response over extended periods. Also, taking into consideration the differential effect of exposure order seen in Jernberg’s study it might be speculated that cell killing can be increased if the stronger DNA damaging modality is applied first. Also, the gammaH2AX results presented here provide supporting evidence that the enhanced cell killing was not mediated by apoptosis. Were the combined treatment to promote apoptosis, the phosphorylation of H2AX would increase in response to DNA fragmentation [29].

Sonication has been shown to affect the cell cycle progression temporarily at earlier time points (first 12h post sonication) and diminish by 24h [34]. This was in harmony with our observations for both cell lines except for U937 cells sonicated at 1Hz. At this PRF, the cells showed significant increase in subG1 peak reaching 22.16±3.19%, while the G2M peak dropped by about 40% compared to the control. Again the increase in subG1 fraction in absence of apoptotic cell death [1] raises scepticism about the interpretation of subG1 cells especially when considering the p53 status of the cells [35]. Interestingly, there was an override of G2M arrest when combined with 1 and 10Hz in U937 cells and with 1Hz in Molt-4 cells. This override was associated with an increase in subG1 cells. These findings draw attention to the potential of 10Hz PRF-US especially that cells were refractory to the bioeffects produced at this PRF alone in terms of membrane and DNA damage. These results may highlight the importance of standing waves as a determinant for cell killing in the combination between US and X-irradiation. No changes were observed at the interim zone reflecting that the effect of US is not associated with the input power solely; rather, it is dependent on the acoustic environment created by the pulse modulation.

In this study, we have shown that under certain conditions, the combination of low-intensity US and X-irradiation could enhance cell killing due to increased membrane damage and ROS formation by US. Although the interaction between both modalities as a factor of increasing PRF and X-ray doses was evaluated as poor in general, it seems that the interaction depends mainly on the acoustic environment determined for each setup and this might explain the contradictory data among reports.
